original_abstract:
The transfer coefficient, $\alpha$, is a fundamental parameter used extensively in the kinetic analysis of electrode processes. According to the 3rd edition of the IUPAC Green Book, the cathodic transfer coefficient $\alpha_c$ is defined as $-(RT/nF)(d\ln k_c/dE)$, where $k_c$ represents the electroreduction rate constant and $E$ is the applied potential. This is equivalent to $-(RT/nF)(d\ln|j_c|/dE)$, where $j_c$ denotes the cathodic current density corrected for mass transport. The anodic coefficient $\alpha_a$ is defined analogously.

This paper demonstrates that these traditional definitions are strictly valid only for electrode reactions involving a single elementary step where $n$ electrons are transferred simultaneously. However, according to Marcus’s theory of electron transfer, the simultaneous exchange of multiple electrons in a single elementary step is physically improbable. Furthermore, the vast majority of electrochemical reactions are complex, multi-step processes rather than single-step events. 

The uncritical use of these definitions has frequently led to flawed mechanistic interpretations. A common error involves equating the experimental quantity $(RT/F)(d\ln|j|/dE)$ with the product $\alpha n$, followed by an attempt to derive the number of electrons ($n$) by assuming an arbitrary value for $\alpha$ (typically 0.5). This derived $n$ is then incorrectly assigned to either a hypothetical rate-determining step or the overall reaction. To remedy these inconsistencies and provide a more robust framework for kinetic analysis, we propose redefining the transfer coefficients as $\alpha_c = -(RT/F)(d\ln|j_c|/dE)$ and $\alpha_a = (RT/F)(d\ln j_a/dE)$. These definitions remain grounded in measurable experimental data and are independent of any a priori mechanistic assumptions.
humanized_abstract:
The cathodic transfer coefficient, αc, and the anodic transfer coefficient, αa, are both important parameters used in the kinetic analysis of electrode processes. The cathodic transfer coefficient, is defined as (IUPAC, 3rd edition: Green Book) (RT/nF) × (d ln kc / dE) or (RT/nF) × (d ln |jc| / dE) where: kc is the rate constant for the electroreduction reaction at the working electrode E is the applied potential at the working electrode jc is the cathodic current density after t (corrected for the effect of mass transport) Likewise, the anodic transfer coefficient, is defined in a similar manner.

The usual expressions for the diffusion-controlled current are applicable only to an electrode reaction composed of a single elementary act involving the transfer of n electrons. But, according to Marcus, the passage of more than one electron in a single act is improbable according to the theory of the electron transfer of Marcus, and the great majority of the electrode reactions are composite and multistage processes.

Definition of transfer coefficients has been used uncritically for many years and as a consequence has led to incorrect conclusions concerning mechanism. The quantity (RT/F) (d ln|j|/dE) has frequently been written as α n and an incorrect value for α has been assumed and the resulting value of n assigned either to the rds or to the overall reaction. It is suggested that the coefficients αc and αa be defined as αc=-(RT/F) (d ln|j c| / dE) and αa=+ (RT/F) (d ln ja / dE) Both of these quantities are determined from direct experimental measurement of kinetic parameters, i.e. no assumption of the mechanism is necessary.

document.input:
The transfer coefficient, $\alpha$, is a fundamental parameter used extensively in the kinetic analysis of electrode processes. According to the 3rd edition of the IUPAC Green Book, the cathodic transfer coefficient $\alpha_c$ is defined as $-(RT/nF)(d\ln k_c/dE)$, where $k_c$ represents the electroreduction rate constant and $E$ is the applied potential. This paper demonstrates that these traditional definitions are strictly valid only for electrode reactions involving a single elementary step where $n$ electrons are transferred simultaneously. A common error involves equating the experimental quantity $(RT/F)(d\ln|j|/dE)$ with the product $\alpha n$, followed by an attempt to derive the number of electrons ($n$) by assuming an arbitrary value for $\alpha$ (typically 0.5). To remedy these inconsistencies and provide a more robust framework for kinetic analysis, we propose redefining the transfer coefficients as $\alpha_c = -(RT/F)(d\ln|j_c|/dE)$ and $\alpha_a = (RT/F)(d\ln j_a/dE)$. These definitions remain grounded in measurable experimental data and are independent of any a priori mechanistic assumptions.
